title-group.article-title: Follow-up of long COVID based on the definition of WHO: a multi-centre cross-sectional questionnaire-based study

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/03et85d35grid.203507.30000 0000 8950 5267Department of Infectious Diseases, The First Affiliated Hospital, Ningbo University, Ningbo, Zhejiang Province China

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Since long COVID has hindered people from normal life, it is essential to understand its full spectrum of manifestation. However, it was heterogeneous in the existing studies and few large-scale studies have been conducted on Asian populations. Here, we conducted a multi-centre questionnaire-based study among Chinese people to explore the long COVID based on the definition of WHO.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: During March 20, 2023 and June 18, 2023, individuals with a history of confirmed or self-reported SARS-CoV-2 infection were recruited from three hospitals to fill out the questionnaire for long COVID. Each symptom was assigned with 0 to 3 points based on their severity. And the long COVID score was a sum of these points of each symptom. The reporting rate, time trend and risk factors of long COVID stratified by different systems were explored.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: 3,693 individuals were recruited for the study. The reporting rate of at least one persistent long COVID symptoms and symptoms impacting daily life was 30.2% (1,117) and 10.7% (394). Systemic symptoms (20.7%, 765) were most easily reported. The most common symptoms were fatigue (16.3%, 602), cough (6.3%, 234) and expectoration (5.5%, 203). The reporting rate of long COVID and long COVID score decreased over time during a 180-day follow-up period (P < 0.05). For long COVID, older age (OR: 1.63, 1.38–1.93), female (OR: 1.19, 1.03–1.38) and SARS-CoV-2 reinfection (OR: 3.56, 2.63–4.80) were risk factors; while number of COVID-19 vaccine doses (OR: 0.87, 0.81–0.94) was a protective factor. The use of traditional Chinese medicine (OR: 0.51, 0.37–0.71) was a protective factor for symptoms impacting daily life.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Early interventions should be taken to minimize the impact of long COVID, especially for the elderly, females and those with SARS-CoV-2 reinfection. COVID-19 booster vaccination might play a potential role in minimizing the impact of long COVID.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12889-025-22671-x.

funding-source.institution: Academy Level Project of the First Affiliated Hospital of Ningbo University
Zhejiang Province Traditional Chinese Medicine Science and Technology Project

award-group.award-id: Evaluation of the effect of traditional Chinese medicine on the elderly patients with severe coronavirus disease 2019

funding-source.institution: Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
Youth Science and Technology Innovation Leading Talent project of Ningbo

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2025

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) is caused by Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), which is still prevalent worldwide. Studies have shown that COVID-19 is not only an acute event, but may lead to a range of long-term symptoms, that is, long COVID [1]. There is no unified definition of long COVID. The World Health Organization (WHO) defines long COVID as the conditions occur in individuals with a history of probable or confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection, usually 3 months from the onset of COVID-19 with symptoms that last for at least 2 months and cannot be explained by an alternative diagnosis [2].

The potential mechanisms of long COVID remain poorly understood, but the current hypothesis involves SARS-CoV-2 persistence, direct organ damage, immune dysregulation, latent virus reactivation, procoagulatory effects, endothelial dysfunction, and microbiome disturbances [3]. Long COVID is a multi-organ disease with a wide range of manifestations, including but not limited to fatigue, shortness of breath and cognitive dysfunction [2]. Due to the diversity of symptoms and the lack of unified evaluation criteria for long COVID, the prevalence of long COVID is controversial [4, 5], but it is generally agreed to be over 10% of all SARS-CoV-2 infections [6].

Long COVID has hindered people from normal life and brought medical and economic challenges. Thus, it is essential to establish a comprehensive understanding of long COVID. Long COVID might be related to several risk factors, including demographics, clinical characteristics, COVID-19 vaccination and SARS-CoV-2 variants [4]. However, the risk factors examined across studies are heterogeneous, which potentially obscures our understanding of long COVID [4]. A study showed that there were significant regional differences in the prevalence of long COVID, which was 51% in Asia, 44% in Europe and 31% in North America [7]. Most studies were based on samples of populations from Europe and North America, and few large-scale studies have been conducted on Asian populations. Therefore, efforts are still needed to examine how COVID plays out in Asia and whether it differs from other geographical area in terms of risk factors and symptoms. This will improve our ability to identify those patients who have a high risk of developing long COVID and make more timely diagnoses.

In this study, we recruited individuals with a history of confirmed or self-reported SARS-CoV-2 infection from three hospitals and conducted a multi-centre cross-sectional questionnaire survey. We explored the reporting rate, time trend and risk factors of long COVID stratified by different systems, in order to provide new evidence.

sec.title: Study design and participants
sec.p: A questionnaire survey was conducted in the outpatient clinics of the First Affiliated Hospital of Ningbo University, Wangchun Community Hospital and Ximen Community Hospital in Haishu District, Ningbo City, Zhejiang Province, China. All patients who visited these three health service centers between March 20, 2023 and June 18, 2023 were invited, and those who agreed would fill out the long COVID questionnaire. In this study with no age limit, persons previously with positive RT-PCR or antigen results of SARS-CoV-2 and those with self-reported with SARS-CoV-2 infection were eligible. We adopt the definition of long COVID used by the WHO: the conditions occur in individuals with a history of probable or confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection, usually 3 months from the onset of COVID-19 with symptoms that last for at least 2 months and cannot be explained by an alternative diagnosis. Therefore, the interval time (referred to the interval between the initial SARS-CoV-2 infection time and the follow-up time in this article) must exceed 3 months. Persons who refused to participate or were unable to cooperate in completing the questionnaire were excluded. The duplicated questionnaires and questionnaires with logic error were excluded in the final analysis.

Before conducting the questionnaire survey, patients were informed of the research purpose, and oral informed consent was obtained from all participants. The Ethical approval was granted by the First Affiliated Hospital of Ningbo University ethical committee (2023004A01). For patients who could use smart devices, the questionnaire survey was distributed in the form of an electronic questionnaire, which was then submitted to the backend of the electronic data management website. For elderly patients or those could not use smart devices, experienced physicians would fill out the paper questionnaire after face-to-face interview and timely input the results of the paper questionnaire into the electronic questionnaire.

sec.title: Long COVID questionnaire
sec.p: A standardized questionnaire was designed, which contained the following information: demography information (age, sex, retirement status, smoking history and drinking history), number of COVID-19 vaccine doses, date of the initial SARS-CoV-2 infection, date of filling out the questionnaire, disease severity of initial SARS-CoV-2 infection (asymptomatic infection, mild infection, moderate infection, severe infection and critical infection), comorbidities before SARS-CoV-2 infection, SARS-CoV-2 reinfection, traditional Chinese medicine use and the assessment of long COVID symptoms. SARS-CoV-2 infection severity criteria were based on the criteria of the 10th trial edition of the Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Treatment of COVID-19 in China [8]. (i) Asymptomatic infection: Patients do not show any relevant clinical symptoms, but have positive pathogenic results of SARS-CoV-2 in respiratory tract or other samples. (ii) Mild infection: Upper respiratory tract infection is the primary symptom, presenting with dry throat, sore throat, cough, fever and similar symptoms. (iii) Moderate infection: Symptoms persist for more than three days, accompanied by cough, shortness of breath and other symptoms. The respiratory rate (RR) remains below 30 breaths times per minute, and the resting-state finger oxygen saturation is above 93%. Imaging reveals manifestations of COVID-19 infection pneumonia. (iv) Severe infection: Shortness of breath is present, with a resting RR above 30 breaths per minute. The peripheral capillary oxygen saturation (SpO2) concentration decreases to less than 93% after air inhalation, oxygenation index arterial oxygen partial pressure/fraction of inspiration oxygen (PaO2/FiO2) is below 300 mmHg, and the patient’s clinical symptoms and lung imaging results progressively worse, with significant lesions exceeding 50% within 24–48 h. (v) Critical infection: Respiratory failure occurs, requiring mechanical ventilation; shock is present; and other organ failures requiring intensive care unit (ICU) monitoring. The severe/critical COVID-19 included severe infection and critical infection in this study.

On the basis of the definition of long COVID by WHO, the self-reported long COVID symptoms recommended by WHO, and the long COVID symptoms reported in other studies, we developed the long COVID questionnaire for this study. The assessment of long COVID symptoms mainly involved five aspects: systemic symptoms, cardiopulmonary symptoms, digestive system symptoms, neurological symptoms and psychiatric symptoms. Systemic symptoms included fatigue, chill, fever, hyperhidrosis, muscle soreness, arthralgia, limbs numbness, rash and alopecia. Cardiopulmonary symptoms included throat pain, cough, expectoration, nasal obstruction, runny nose, chest pain, dyspnea (at rest, during exercise and during sleep), shortness of breath, chest tightness and palpitation. The digestive system symptoms included dysphagia, anorexia, nausea, vomiting, dyspepsia, constipation and diarrhea. Neurological symptoms included dizziness, headache, memory deterioration, brain fog, expression disorders, understanding disorders, ageusia and anosmia. The brain fog refers to the short-term memory loss, difficulty concentrating and confusion in thinking. In daily life, patients often feel that they need to spend more time and energy to complete the same tasks [9, 10, 11]. Psychiatric symptoms included insomnia, anxiety, loneliness, sadness, decreased interest, hopelessness and self-harm.

The assessment of symptoms in this study was conducted using a question, such as “Have you consistently experienced fatigue in the past two months”. The severity of each symptom was divided into 0–3 levels (None, Minor, Obvious and Severe), and the patients chose the options based on their own experience. As mentioned above, a total of 43 long COVID symptoms were evaluated in this study. Therefore, the total score of long COVID ranged from 0 to 129. Persons with at least one persistent long COVID symptom were considered to have long COVID [12, 13, 14]. We also asked patients whether these symptoms impacted daily life through one question. Participants were asked if they had developed new comorbidity following SARS-CoV-2 infection.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Two researchers independently checked the raw data and filtered out unqualified data. The characteristics of the participants, such as sex, retirement status, smoking history, etc., were provided as absolute numbers with concomitant percentages. Variables such as age, interval time and score were represented by median with interquartile range (IQR). ROC curve was used to calculate the cutoff value of the long COVID score for symptoms impacting daily life. The Pearson correlation was used to evaluate the time trends of the reporting rate and score of long COVID. In this study, the interval time of the population was mainly concentrated within 6 months (3394/3693, 91.9%) (Supplementary Fig. 1). After 6 months, the population base at each time point was small, which was easily affected by molecular size and caused data instability. Therefore, the time trend was explored to 180 days.

To explore the risk factors of long COVID, logistic regression analysis was conducted to estimate the odds ratios (ORs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs). According to the previous studies and the actual situation of this study, the following variables were included in the model: age, sex, number of COVID-19 vaccine doses, disease severity of initial SARS-CoV-2 infection, comorbidities before SARS-CoV-2 infection, SARS-CoV-2 reinfection and traditional Chinese medicine use. Due to the missing values for SARS-CoV-2 reinfection variables and traditional Chinese medicine use in some individuals, we employed multivariate imputation for missing values before conducting the regression analysis. The univariate logistic regression analysis was first performed, and the variables with P value < 0.05 were then included in the multivariate logistic regression analysis to reduce confounding. Chi-squared test was used to compare the difference of long COVID reporting rate among subgroups. All tests were two-sided, and a P value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant. The data cleaning and analysis were performed using the Microsoft Excel 2016 and R version 3.2.3.

sec.title: Characteristics of participants
sec.p: From March 20, 2023 to June 18, 2023, a total of 4,194 questionnaires were received, of which 30 were excluded by duplicates, 242 were excluded due to low quality, and 229 were excluded by interval time less than 3 months. Finally, 3,693 cases were included in the final analysis (Fig. 1).

Table 1 summarized the characteristics of included participants. The median age was 63 years (IQR: 41–68), and 55.8% (2,060/3,693) were female. 45.8% (1,690/3,693) of participants had comorbidities before SARS-CoV-2 infection, and hypertension was the most common comorbidity (Supplementary Fig. 2). 8.2% (301/3,693) of participants had not been vaccinated against COVID-19, 0.3% (10/3,693) were diagnosed as severe/critical COVID-19, and 3.7% (47/1,268) had been reinfected by SARS-CoV-2. The median interval time between the initial infection time and the follow-up time was 134 days (IQR: 110–161).

table-wrap-foot.p: Interval time, the interval between the initial SARS-CoV-2 infection time and the follow-up time

caption.p: The flowchart of this study. Interval time, the interval between the initial SARS-CoV-2 infection time and the follow-up time

sec.title: Long COVID symptoms among participants
sec.p: The reporting rate of participants with at least one COVID symptom was 30.2% (1,117/3,693), and their median score of long COVID was 2 (IQR: 1–4). 15.4% (569/3,693) reported 1 symptom with median score of long COVID of 1 (IQR: 1–1), 5.6% (205/3,693) reported 2 symptoms with median score of long COVID of 2 (IQR: 2–2), and 9.3% (343/3,693) reported 3 or more symptoms with median score of long COVID of 8 (IQR: 4–17). Female patients had a higher percentage of long COVID (female vs. male: 32.2% vs. 27.8%, P < 0.05) (Table 1, Supplementary Table 1 and Supplementary Fig. 3).

Systemic symptoms (20.7%, 765/3,693) were most easily reported. Female patients had significantly higher percentages of digestive system symptoms (5.5% vs. 4.0%, P < 0.05). The most common symptoms were fatigue (16.3%, 602/3,693), cough (6.3%, 234/3,693), expectoration (5.5%, 203/3,693), alopecia (5.2%, 191/3,693), arthralgia (5.1%, 187/3,693) and throat pain (5.1%, 187/3,693). For the specific symptoms, female patients had significantly higher percentages of chest tightness (1.6% vs. 0.6%, P < 0.05), dyspnea during exercise (5.8% vs. 3.9%, P < 0.05), palpitation (1.6% vs. 0.4%, P < 0.05), dyspepsia (1.0% vs. 0.4%, P < 0.05), vomiting (0.5% vs. 0.1%, P < 0.05), diarrhea (1.5% vs. 0.6%, P < 0.05), constipation (3.3% vs. 2.1%, P < 0.05), dizziness (2.7% vs. 0.9%, P < 0.05), expression disorders (1.0% vs. 0.3%, P < 0.05) and anxiety (1.5% vs. 0.6%, P < 0.05) (Supplementary Table 1 and Fig. 2).

caption.p: The reporting rate of long COVID stratified by different systems

sec.p: 10.7% (394/3,693) of participants reported that symptoms impacted daily life, and their score of long COVID was higher (P < 0.05) (Table 1; Fig. 2). When the total score was greater than 13, it was more likely to suffer from symptoms that impacted daily life (P < 0.05) (Supplementary Fig. 4). After SARS-CoV-2 infection, 1.4% (52/3,693) were newly diagnosed with comorbidities, mainly hypertension (Table 1 and Supplementary Table 2).

The reporting rate of long COVID, systemic symptoms, cardiopulmonary symptoms, digestive system symptoms and psychiatric symptoms decreased over time during a 180-day follow-up period (P < 0.05). The reporting rate of symptoms impacting daily life and neurological symptoms did not show a statistical time trend (P > 0.05) (Figs. 3 and 4).

caption.p: Time trend of reporting rate and score of long COVID during a 180-day follow-up period. (A) Time trend of reporting rate of long COVID; (B) Time trend of reporting rate of symptoms impacting daily life; (C) Time trend of long COVID score among participants reporting at least one long COVID symptom; (D) Time trend of long COVID score among participants reporting symptoms impacting daily life

sec.p: The long COVID score of participants with at least one symptom and participants with symptoms impacting daily life decreased over time during a 180-day follow-up period (P < 0.05). The scores of digestive symptoms, neurological symptoms and psychiatric symptoms decreased over time during a 180-day follow-up period (P < 0.05). The scores of systemic symptoms increased over time during a 180-day follow-up period (P < 0.05). The scores of cardiopulmonary symptoms did not show a statistical time trend (P > 0.05) (Figs. 3 and 4).

caption.p: Time trend of reporting rate and score stratified by different systems during a 180-day follow-up period. A-E. Time trend of reporting rate of long COVID symptoms stratified by different systems; F-J. Time trend of score stratified by different systems

sec.title: Risk factors of long COVID
sec.p: For long COVID, based on the univariate logistic regression model, age, sex, number of COVID-19 vaccine doses, comorbidity and SARS-CoV-2 reinfection status were the potential influencing factors (Supplementary Table 3). When introducing the above variables into multivariate logistic regression model, older age (OR: 1.63, 1.38–1.93), female (OR: 1.19, 1.03–1.38) and SARS-CoV-2 reinfection (OR: 3.56, 2.63–4.80) were risk factors; while number of COVID-19 vaccine doses (OR: 0.87, 0.81–0.94) was a protective factor (Fig. 5).

For symptoms impacting daily life, based on the univariate logistic regression model, age, number of COVID-19 vaccine doses and traditional Chinese medicine treatment were the potential influencing factors (Supplementary Table 4). When introducing the above variables into multivariate logistic regression model, older age (OR: 3.13, 2.48–3.95) was a risk factor; while number of COVID-19 vaccine doses (OR: 0.90, 0.82–1.00) and the use of traditional Chinese medicine (OR: 0.51, 0.37–0.71) were protective factors (Fig. 5).

Supplementary Tables 5 to 9 showed the logistic analysis results of long COVID symptoms stratified by different systems. Since hypertension and Type 2 diabetes were the most common comorbidities in this study population, we also calculated their ORs (Table 2). We found that hypertension was a risk factor of long COVID (OR: 1.23, 1.07–1.42), systemic symptoms (OR: 1.25, 1.06–1.47) and cardiopulmonary symptoms (OR: 1.75, 1.44–2.11). Type 2 diabetes was the risk factor of long COVID (OR: 1.43, 1.19–1.72), symptoms impacting daily life (OR: 2.12, 1.66–2.71), systemic symptoms (OR: 1.55, 1.26–1.90) and cardiopulmonary symptoms (OR: 2.68, 2.16–3.34).

caption.p: The multivariate logistic regression results of long COVID and symptoms impacting daily life. *, P < 0.05
Odds ratios of hypertension and type 2 diabetes for long COVID stratified by different systems

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Counting long COVID cases remains complex due to the use of different methodologies and definitions, the prevalence of long COVID varied across studies, from below 10% to around 60% [6, 7, 15, 16]. In this multi-centre questionnaire-based study involving 3,693 individuals with SARS-CoV-2 infection history, the reporting rate of long COVID was 30.2%. In this study, we adopt the definition of long COVID used by the WHO and assessed whether patients had persistent long COVID symptoms in the past 2 months, which might be one of the reasons for the low reporting rate of this study compared with other researches. In addition, the participants included in this study were mainly non-hospitalized patients with COVID-19 (almost 80% of the participants were asymptomatic infection or mild infection). A meta-analysis study comprising a total of 1,680,003 COVID-19 positive patients reported the estimated pooled prevalence of long COVID at 43% (95% CI: 39 − 46%), affecting 54% (95% CI: 44 − 63%) of hospitalized patients and 34% (95% CI: 25 − 46%) of non-hospitalized patients [7].

It is well confirmed that fatigue is one of the most common symptoms reported by COVID-19 survivors [17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22]. We found the most common symptom was fatigue with a reporting rate of 16.3%, which was low compared to most studies. The possible reasons were discussed above. However, the results of some studies were similar to ours. A 6-month retrospective cohort study of 273,618 COVID-19 survivors found that the reporting rate of fatigue in 6 months after COVID-19 diagnosis was 12.82% [23]. In a telephone questionnaire study conducted in Russia, persistent fatigue was reported by 561 (21.2%) participants 6 to 8 months after discharge from hospital with COVID-19 [15]. Fatigue is common after acute lung injury and is associated with substantial impairments in physical function and health-related quality of life [24]. In addition to SARS-CoV-2 infection, chronic fatigue was also found after SARS, Middle East Respiratory Syndrome (MERS) and Ebola virus infection [25, 26, 27, 28], which indicated that COVID-19 and other infectious diseases with chronic fatigue in the recovery might share a common mechanism.

The risk factors of long COVID are still under exploration. In our study, older age was the risk factor with OR of 1.63 (95% CI: 1.38–1.93) for long COVID and OR of 3.13 (95% CI: 2.48–3.95) for symptoms impacting daily life. Although some studies pointed out that the risk of developing long COVID was found to be increased along a gradient of decreasing age [29], many studies have shown that the elderly were the high-risk group for long COVID [30, 31, 32]. A study involving 4,182 SARS-CoV-2 infections found that the long COVID prevalence was significantly associated with age, rising from 9.9% in the individuals aged 18–49 years to 21.9% in those aged ≥ 70 years [32].

Compared with male individuals, female patients had a significantly higher percentage of long COVID (32.2% vs. 27.8%, P < 0.05), with the OR of 1.19 (95% CI: 1.03–1.38). And female patients were more likely to suffer from chest tightness, dyspnea during exercise, palpitation, dyspepsia, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, dizziness, expression disorders and anxiety. Studies pointed that female sex was the risk factor for long COVID [20, 29, 33]. In addition to biological differences of sex hormones and genotype [34], women might be more aware of bodily sensations than men, allowing for an easier and earlier perception of somatic symptoms in women than in men [35]. SARS-CoV-2 reinfection was the risk factor of long COVID in our study, with the OR of 3.56 (95% CI: 2.63–4.80). A recent cohort study with large-sample showed that compared to no reinfection, reinfection contributed additional risks of long COVID (hazard ratio: 2.10, 2.04–2.16) [36].

It is known that traditional Chinese medicine has played a significant role in the fight against COVID-19 in China [37]. In this study, we found that the use of traditional Chinese medicine (OR: 0.51, 0.37–0.71) was a protective factor for preventing the occurrence of symptoms impacting daily life. A multi-centre, double-blind, randomized controlled trial found that the Bufei Huoxue could exert strong rehabilitative effects on physiological activity in patients recovering from COVID-19, attenuating symptoms of fatigue and improving exercise tolerance [38]. Another randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multi-centre clinical trial showed that Jinshuibao Tablets and Shengmaiyin Oral Liquid significantly improved the cardiopulmonary function of patients. Shumian capsule, Xiangsha Liujunwan and Dangshen Oral Liquid could significantly improve patients’ sleep disorder and digestive function respectively [39]. Traditional Chinese medicine plays a potential therapeutic role in the recovery period of COVID-19 by eliminating inflammation, reducing lung damage and regulating immunity through multiple targets and pathways [40, 41].

Our study found that the number of COVID-19 vaccine doses (OR: 0.87, 0.81–0.94) was a protective factor to prevent long COVID. Data from the UK Office for National Statistics showed that in a sample of UK adults aged 18 to 69 years, receiving two doses of a COVID-19 vaccine at least two weeks before the first SARS-CoV-2 infection was associated with a 41.1% decrease in the odds of self-reported long COVID at least 12 weeks later, relative to socio-demographically similar study participants who were not vaccinated when infected [42]. But there was also an article that pointed out that vaccination did not improve symptoms of long COVID [43], which might be due to various confounding factors, such as the vaccine types, vaccination interval and breakthrough infection.

There is an undeniable fact that the existing research results on long COVID are highly heterogeneous, which has hindered our understanding of long COVID to some extent [4]. Therefore, it is of great importance to swiftly develop norms for long COVID to reduce heterogeneity, such as core outcome events (especially symptoms with priority evaluation), validated evaluation tools, widely recognized symptom questionnaires and specific follow-up time points [19].

This study has several limitations. It was a questionnaire survey, and all symptoms were self-reported by patients and could not be objectively assessed. This study focused on the prevalence and specific symptoms of long COVID, but it is not yet clear whether patients with long COVID were accompanied with organ dysfunction. Accessing whether symptoms impact the quality of life was based on a single question through patients’ subjective feelings, rather than combining with scales based on objective indicators. Future studies should incorporate standardized tools to provide a more comprehensive evaluation of health-related quality of life in patients with long COVID. Meanwhile, the retrospective nature of this study exposed the possibility of recall bias and the inclusion of patients with self-reported infection could also introduce biases. We did not include a control group, nor did investigate whether these symptoms were present also before COVID-19. All of the above might affect the reliability of symptom prevalence. In addition, the sample size of some subgroups was insufficient, which limits the generalizability of the research results in specific populations. It is still necessary to explore the long COVID- from multiple and comprehensive perspectives.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The reporting rate and severity of long COVID decreased with time. Early interventions should be taken to minimize the impact of long COVID, especially for the elderly, females and those with SARS-CoV-2 reinfection. COVID-19 booster vaccination and traditional Chinese medicine might play potential roles in minimizing the impact of long COVID. Standardized longitudinal researches for long COVID are needed to ascertain a better understanding of the condition and its longer-term impact.

sec.title: Electronic supplementary material
sec.p: Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.
Danying Yan and Ying Liu contributed equally to this work and share first authorship.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We appreciate the cooperation of all patients.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: DY-Y and YL wrote the manuscript and performed data analysis. RC and CW-W had taken responsibility of the data integrity. DY-Y took responsibility for the accuracy of data analysis. YL and LZ were responsible for data collection. GQ-Q designed the study. GQ-Q, QF-S, XQ-C and Ma AHY revised the manuscript. All authors contributed to the article and approved the submitted version.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This work was supported by the grants from the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Y23H190011, LTGG23H260002), Zhejiang Province Traditional Chinese Medicine Science and Technology Project (Evaluation of the effect of traditional Chinese medicine on the elderly patients with severe coronavirus disease 2019), Youth Science and Technology Innovation Leading Talent project of Ningbo (2023QL055) and the Academy Level Project of the First Affiliated Hospital of Ningbo University (XGZ2301). The funder did not participate in the process of diagnosis and treatment.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The raw data supporting the conclusion of the manuscript can be available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: The Ethical approval was granted by the First Affiliated Hospital of Ningbo University ethical committee (2023004A01). Informed consent was obtained from all subjects and/or their legal guardian(s).

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

element-citation.article-title: A conceptual framework to accelerate the clinical impact of evolving research into long COVID

element-citation.article-title: Long COVID: pathophysiology, current concepts, and future directions

element-citation.article-title: Long covid-mechanisms, risk factors, and management
